Stressed German Credit Dataset

A stressed variant of the German Credit Dataset with controlled, documented input degradation.

Overview

The Stressed German Credit Dataset (SGCD) is a synthetic benchmark derived from the UCI German Credit Dataset. It republishes the original credit records with controlled, documented input degradation ("shocks") applied to a subset of examples.

Each of the original 1,000 credit records is transformed into a structured example containing both a machine-readable JSON view and a human-readable text description. Controlled perturbations ("shocks") are applied across temporal batches to simulate realistic data quality degradation.

SGCD-v0.1
Version
1,000
Total Samples
20
Batches
70 / 30
G / B Ratio
50
Samples per Batch
3
Phases

Phase Design

The dataset is organized into 20 sequential batches across three phases. The structure is progressive: early batches are clean, later batches carry an increasing rate of perturbation, and the final batches include both previously seen and novel shock types.

PhaseBatches#Batches#Examples Shock RateShock TypesPurpose
SetupS1–S442000 %NoneClean baseline (no shocks)
AdaptationA1–A101050020 %–60 %F1, F2, F3Increasing rate of perturbation
Test (seen)T1–T3315040 % fixedF1, F2, F3Previously seen shock types
Test (unseen)T4–T6315040 % fixedF4Novel shock type (contradiction)

Shock Types

Five types of perturbation are defined. F0 is the baseline (clean). F1–F3 are seen during adaptation; F4 is reserved exclusively for the test-unseen phase.

CodeNameDescription
F0Clean No modification — faithful representation of the original record
F1Missingness One field is dropped from the JSON view (set to null) or omitted from the text view
F2Ambiguity Precise descriptions in the text are replaced with vague or coded alternatives (JSON stays intact)
F3Cosmetic Surface formatting changes: field reordering, pipe delimiters, or raw codes instead of descriptions
F4Contradiction The text states a different (but domain-valid) value than the JSON for one field

Label Encoding

G (Good) = low credit risk  |  B (Bad) = high credit risk. Every batch maintains a fixed 35G / 15B ratio (70 % / 30 %).

Attributes

Each sample contains 20 features from the original German Credit Dataset. 7 are numeric and 13 are categorical. The table below lists each attribute with its code-to-description mapping.

FieldLabelTypeValues / Codes
checking_accountChecking account statusCategorical
  • A11 — < 0 DM
  • A12 — 0 <= ... < 200 DM
  • A13 — >= 200 DM / salary assignments for at least 1 year
  • A14 — no checking account
durationCredit durationNumeric—
credit_historyCredit historyCategorical
  • A30 — no credits taken / all credits paid back duly
  • A31 — all credits at this bank paid back duly
  • A32 — existing credits paid back duly till now
  • A33 — delay in paying off in the past
  • A34 — critical account / other credits existing (not at this bank)
purposePurpose of creditCategorical
  • A40 — car (new)
  • A41 — car (used)
  • A42 — furniture/equipment
  • A43 — radio/television
  • A44 — domestic appliances
  • A45 — repairs
  • A46 — education
  • A47 — vacation
  • A48 — retraining
  • A49 — business
  • A410 — others
credit_amountCredit amountNumeric—
savings_accountSavings accountCategorical
  • A61 — < 100 DM
  • A62 — 100 <= ... < 500 DM
  • A63 — 500 <= ... < 1000 DM
  • A64 — >= 1000 DM
  • A65 — unknown / no savings account
employment_sinceEmployment durationCategorical
  • A71 — unemployed
  • A72 — < 1 year
  • A73 — 1 <= ... < 4 years
  • A74 — 4 <= ... < 7 years
  • A75 — >= 7 years
installment_rateInstallment rateNumeric—
personal_status_sexPersonal statusCategorical
  • A91 — male : divorced/separated
  • A92 — female : divorced/separated/married
  • A93 — male : single
  • A94 — male : married/widowed
  • A95 — female : single
other_debtorsOther debtors/guarantorsCategorical
  • A101 — none
  • A102 — co-applicant
  • A103 — guarantor
residence_sinceResidence durationNumeric—
propertyPropertyCategorical
  • A121 — real estate
  • A122 — building society savings agreement / life insurance
  • A123 — car or other
  • A124 — unknown / no property
ageAgeNumeric—
other_installment_plansOther installment plansCategorical
  • A141 — bank
  • A142 — stores
  • A143 — none
housingHousingCategorical
  • A151 — rent
  • A152 — own
  • A153 — for free
num_existing_creditsNumber of existing creditsNumeric—
jobJobCategorical
  • A171 — unemployed / unskilled - non-resident
  • A172 — unskilled - resident
  • A173 — skilled employee / official
  • A174 — management / self-employed / highly qualified employee / officer
num_dependentsNumber of dependentsNumeric—
telephoneTelephoneCategorical
  • A191 — none
  • A192 — yes, registered under the customers name
foreign_workerForeign workerCategorical
  • A201 — yes
  • A202 — no

Shock Distribution

Stacked count of each semantic shock type by phase. Setup is entirely clean (F0). Adaptation introduces F1 and F2. Test-seen uses F1+F2; test-unseen uses F4.

Temporal evolution of shock type counts across the 20 sequential batches.

Percentage of semantically perturbed (non-F0) samples in each batch. Shows 0% in setup, an increasing rate during adaptation, and a fixed 40% in test.
